title: How Many Murders? How Many Robberies?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. This letter compiles reported crimes, court observations and allegations to challenge the government's law-and-order claims; the translation preserves that attribution. Source comparison confirms Justice K. Subbiah's name and repairs including `கொடூரமாக`, `சாட்சியங்களின்`, `விஜயா`, `திருவைகுண்டத்தில்`, `கற்பழிக்கப்பட்டுக்`, `இரண்டு பேர்`, `அருகில்`, `உத்தரவிட்டார்`, `அனுமதிக்கப்பட்டுள்ளது`, `மீது`, and `என்றால்`. These changes are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy. The printed salutation is restored; canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: Last week, a three-day conference of District Collectors and police officers was held in Chennai under the Chief Minister's leadership. Jayalalithaa addressed it on all three days. Newspapers prominently reported what she said about law and order in her opening address:

> As far as law and order is concerned, it is in a condition of which everyone present can be proud and satisfied. Tamil Nadu is a state without communal, extremist or religious conflict. This has been possible only because I allowed the police and district administrations to function independently, without imposing any obstacles.

At the District Collectors' conference on 17 December last year, she similarly said: “Law, order and peace in Tamil Nadu are maintained better than in any other state. There is no terrorism in Tamil Nadu, and communalism has not been allowed to raise its head.”
At an event the very next day, I listed with statistics how many murders, robberies, chain snatchings and highway robberies had occurred under the AIADMK government until then.
I believe a reminder of just a few incidents will show the true state of law and order under this government.

Last November, AIADMK MLA Karuppaiah of Sholavandan escaped with his life by locking himself inside a hotel room, frightened by a gang led by an AIADMK union secretary that came to attack him with wooden clubs near Vadipatti. Is this another sign of excellent law and order under AIADMK rule?
Two months ago, High Court Justices Jayachandran and Nagamuthu said in their judgment in a murder case:

> The police seized the sickle used in the murder but did not produce it before the court. They did not send the bloodstain on it for DNA testing and prove that it was the weapon used by the killers. There were several defects in the investigation. The police were given an opportunity to cure those defects, but they failed to use it properly. The many shortcomings, mistakes and incompetence of the police have been exposed in this case. We do not know how the investigating agency can be criticised any further. > > Hard-hearted killers brutally murdered four people, yet the entire police investigation has gone to waste. The police have destroyed the confidence placed in them by the High Court. They failed to prove the case through proper evidence and testimony. They must recognise their failure in responsibility and duty. Police negligence in investigation causes justice and fairness to be defeated. With a heavy heart, we say that justice has been defeated in this case.
Who else could praise the police under Jayalalithaa's control more eloquently than these judges?

During the first week of November, newspapers reported that nine people were murdered in a single day in Tiruchi, Thanjavur and Nagapattinam alone. Is that too a tribute to law and order under this government?

On 20 November, in a busy area opposite the police outpost at Nelpettai in Madurai, a bomb planted by unidentified persons exploded in High Court advocate Akbar Ali's car. Fortunately, the car was parked and no one died. That too must be a sign of sound law and order!

Puzhal prison has capacity for 1,250 inmates, but now holds 2,381. Of them, 1,061 were detained under the Goondas Act and National Security Act. Is this not another example of excellent law and order in Tamil Nadu?

When the DMDK sought permission for a public meeting in Cuddalore last November to explain its general-council resolutions, the police denied it on law-and-order grounds. The party challenged that denial in the High Court. Justice Sasidharan ruled that it was wrong to deny permission on those grounds, that protecting a public meeting was the police's duty, and that permission must be granted.

Jayalalithaa says Tamil Nadu has no communal clashes. Yet after she came to power, six people were killed in the police firing at Paramakudi on 11 September 2011. Although the High Court ordered a CBI inquiry on 23 December 2011, the CBI did not investigate it.

The Madurai Collector barred Dr Ramadoss from entering the district; the Cuddalore Collector issued a similar order; Viduthalai Chiruthaigal leader Thol. Thirumavalavan was barred from entering Villupuram district; and prohibitory orders under Section 144 were imposed throughout Dharmapuri district. Were these orders suppressing democratic activity also signs of excellent law and order?
Consider the reported cases of sexual violence against women in recent months:

* Nine-year-old Gayathri of Andiyappan village near Tirupattur in Vellore district was raped and murdered. * Nandini of Sivagiri was raped and murdered. * The body of a woman named Vijaya was found in Saidapet. * Thirteen-year-old schoolgirl Punitha of Srivaikuntam in Thoothukudi district was raped and murdered. * An eleven-year-old Dalit girl in Nagapattinam district was raped by two men. * A young woman named Suganthi was raped on the bank of the Manimuthar in Vriddhachalam. * Sandhya, a young woman from Manjakuzhi village near Chidambaram, was raped and thrown from the third floor into the street. * Menaka, a married young woman from Thatharavalasai in Harur taluk, Dharmapuri, was raped and murdered. * Mariammal, a young woman living with her father and three children after separating from her husband in Thoothukudi's Matha Nagar Second Street, was raped by intruders in the early morning. * Kalki, a schoolgirl from Rukmanipuram in Vanur, Villupuram, was raped and her body hung in a casuarina grove. * A twelve-year-old girl in Udumalai was sexually assaulted and, unable to bear the humiliation, set herself on fire. * An eighteen-year-old woman in Namakkal was raped by a four-member gang near the Collectorate. * Seventeen-year-old Abhinaya of Timiri near Arcot in Vellore district set herself on fire and died to protect herself after threats and intimidation. * Premalatha of Pozhichalur, Chennai, was raped and murdered, and her body thrown into a forest. * Meena, a married young woman near Thottiyam in Tiruchi district, was abducted in a car and raped by four men while attending a relative's wedding. * An eleven-year-old sixth-standard girl at Pudur in Orathanadu was raped while grazing goats. * A girl was murdered near Ranipet and found burned in a cremation ground.
Cruelty against women continues without restraint. Is this the proud Tamil state acclaimed for maintaining law and order?

On 30 January 2013, the party applied for police permission to hold a Salem parliamentary-constituency workers' meeting on 16 February, at which party Treasurer Thalapathi M.K. Stalin would deliver a special address. Permission was denied. The DMK went to the Salem Principal Sessions Court. Judge Bhuvaneswari ordered that no one should obstruct the meeting and rejected the police request for a ban because proper permission had been sought and rules followed.

This was not the first incident. The AIADMK government barred a meeting in Karur on 15 December 2012 at which younger brother Stalin was to participate. A case was filed in the Madurai Bench of the High Court on 11 December. Justice K. Subbiah permitted the meeting at the same venue.

Thalapathi Stalin was due to attend a parliamentary-constituency workers' meeting in Thoothukudi on 28 December 2012. At the ruling party's instigation, permission was denied there too. The matter went to court, where Justice Ravichandra Babu ruled:
> Many public meetings have already been permitted at the requested venue. The Corporation has designated it specifically for public meetings. There is no basis for refusing the petitioner's request.
He permitted the meeting at that location. Are these incidents denying the fundamental democratic rights of speech and assembly examples of maintaining law and order?

It is not only party events. Was permission granted for Superstar Rajinikanth's birthday programme? For actor Vijay's birthday meeting? Why, was permission granted for the TESO conference? No. Permission had to be obtained through the courts. Are these proofs of sound law and order?

Police officers were accused of raping four tribal women near Tirukkoyilur. One survivor complained to the Villupuram Superintendent of Police. Advocate Pugalenthi petitioned the High Court, saying: “No action has been taken against the police officers accused of rape. The case must be transferred to the CBI.”
The petition came before the First Bench comprising Chief Justice Iqbal and Justice Sivagnanam. The Bench said:
> Why is the law applicable to ordinary people not enforced against police officers? Compensation cannot restore what was lost. The government must take legal action against those concerned.
Another example of the excellent maintenance of law and order!
Under the headline “Sixteen Murders in Chennai in One Month,” *Daily Thanthi* reported:

> Sixteen murders have taken place in Chennai in a single month. The accused in five of them have not yet been arrested. The police are struggling to solve the sensational murder of a woman bank officer in Nungambakkam.
This too must be a sign of excellent law-and-order maintenance!
Here is another certificate issued by the court to the police under Jayalalithaa's control:

> The Madras High Court Bench here came down heavily on police officers “who think they are a law unto themselves just because they are vested with certain powers under the Code of Criminal Procedure and that the common people are at their mercy”.
That appeared in *The Hindu* on 14 February 2012.

The Chief Minister proudly cited the arrest of “Police” Fakruddin as evidence of police performance. But the same police now say Fakruddin confessed to murdering a particular person whom, only months earlier, they said had been murdered by several others. They arrested those people, took action and sent them to prison. Was that not also the police under Jayalalithaa's leadership? Who will praise this contradiction? Lower-ranking officers face action for the mistake, but should not the Chief Minister responsible for the department explain it? Praise goes only to the Chief Minister, while punishment goes to officers below?

The Chief Minister also said the police and district administrations had complete freedom to suppress anti-social forces with an iron hand. In truth, they use that iron hand not against anti-social forces but against people they dislike and leaders of political parties they oppose. The countless defamation cases are evidence.

There would be neither enough time nor newspaper space to list every incident showing that law and order has not been properly maintained under this government. Yet whether at a Collectors' conference or in reply to the debate on the Governor's Address, Jayalalithaa routinely claims Tamil Nadu is a proud state in maintaining law and order, and newspapers print the claim in banner headlines. Does a falsehood become true merely because it is repeatedly proclaimed and publicised?

The evidence of law and order under Jayalalithaa's government is this: during these two and a half years, newspapers alone reported 3,231 murders, 1,170 robberies, 691 highway robberies and frauds, and 652 chain snatchings. This is the mark of how well law and order is maintained under Jayalalithaa's rule!
With affection,
M.K.
